title: Namespaces and qualified names

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Namespaces and qualified names; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. canonicalExplanation.summary: Namespaces organize names and control lookup without creating runtime objects; qualified names make ownership explicit and using declarations import selected names into a scope.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A library exposes learn::parse and the caller uses the qualified name, avoiding collision with another parse function. Your goal is to resolve and correct ambiguous or hidden names using explicit qualification. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf computing execution; pf tools terminal editor; Program structure and main. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: Namespaces organize names and control lookup without creating runtime objects; qualified names make ownership explicit and using declarations import selected names into a scope. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Argument-dependent lookup, inline and anonymous namespaces, aliases, and broad using directives can change lookup or linkage in non-obvious ways. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: using namespace creates a copy of that namespace's objects and is harmless in public headers.

includes: Use namespaces to organize APIs and avoid broad using-directives in headers.
Resolve and correct ambiguous or hidden names using explicit qualification.
excludes: Argument-dependent lookup, inline and anonymous namespaces, aliases, and broad using directives can change lookup or linkage in non-obvious ways.

outcomes: Resolve and correct ambiguous or hidden names using explicit qualification.

examples.title: Namespaces and qualified names: defined case
examples.situation: A library exposes learn::parse and the caller uses the qualified name, avoiding collision with another parse function.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A library exposes learn::parse and the caller uses the qualified name, avoiding collision with another parse function.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: A using directive affects name lookup, not object creation; avoid broad directives in headers and prefer qualification or narrow using declarations.
examples.result: The defined result follows because A using directive affects name lookup, not object creation; avoid broad directives in headers and prefer qualification or narrow using declarations.

examples.title: Namespaces and qualified names: boundary case
examples.situation: A header avoids using namespace std because it would inject many names into every including translation unit.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A header avoids using namespace std because it would inject many names into every including translation unit.
Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Argument-dependent lookup, inline and anonymous namespaces, aliases, and broad using directives can change lookup or linkage in non-obvious ways.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: using namespace creates a copy of that namespace's objects and is harmless in public headers.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Resolve and correct ambiguous or hidden names using explicit qualification.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: using namespace creates a copy of that namespace's objects and is harmless in public headers.
misconceptions.correction: A using directive affects name lookup, not object creation; avoid broad directives in headers and prefer qualification or narrow using declarations.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: using namespace creates a copy of that namespace's objects and is harmless in public headers.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Namespaces and qualified names: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Argument-dependent lookup, inline and anonymous namespaces, aliases, and broad using directives can change lookup or linkage in non-obvious ways.
